title: Pappardelle ai porcini
subtitle: Pappardelle with porcini and parsley in olive oil
excerpt: Wide pappardelle ribbons tossed with reconstituted porcini, garlic, and parsley in olive oil, no cream, no cheese in the sauce. The Tuscan version of the porcini pasta, drier and more intense than the cream variant, the cooking liquid reduced to a concentrated glaze.
sourceNotes: Based on the Tuscan pasta ai porcini tradition documented in Giuliano Bugialli, 'The Fine Art of Italian Cooking' (1977) and Marcella Hazan, 'Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking' (1992). The olive oil version is the older preparation; the cream variant is a more recent adaptation.

content.text: The Emilian version of porcini pasta uses cream. The Tuscan version uses olive oil and the dark soaking liquid from the dried mushrooms. The result is sharper, darker, and more intense. No dairy gets in the way of the porcini. The soaking liquid cooks down to almost nothing. It leaves a dark coat on the mushrooms. Pasta water then loosens it into a sauce.

Pappardelle is wide enough to hold the sauce. It is sturdy enough to stand up to the strong mushroom flavour. Fresh pappardelle, if you can find it, cooks in 2 to 3 minutes. The texture is softer. Dried pappardelle takes a little longer. It stands up to tossing without breaking.

items.name: Dried porcini
items.prepNote: soaked in 300 ml warm water for 20 minutes

content.text: Soak {{dried-porcini}} of dried porcini in 300 ml of warm water for 20 minutes. Lift out, squeeze gently, and chop roughly. Strain the soaking liquid carefully through a fine sieve, leaving the last tablespoon in the bowl.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{garlic}} sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. When the garlic begins to colour, add the mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes. Pour in the strained porcini liquid. Simmer until reduced by two-thirds, the liquid should be almost gone and the mushrooms glazed. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Cook {{pappardelle}} of pappardelle in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a large mug of pasta water, then drain. Add the pasta to the mushroom pan over a medium heat with 4 to 5 tablespoons of pasta water. Toss vigorously to coat. Add most of the {{parsley}} chopped parsley and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Toss once more. Serve with the remaining parsley and {{parmesan}} parmesan at the table.

items.fix: Add pasta water a tablespoon at a time, the starch emulsifies with the olive oil and coats the pasta rather than making it wet.
Reduce heat when the liquid goes in. It should simmer, not boil away in 30 seconds.
items.cause: Heat too high when the liquid was added

content.text: Porcini pasta in olive oil is the older Tuscan version. It belongs to the hill towns south of Florence, where the forests yield porcini from late summer. The cream version is from Emilia and came later. It is now better known outside Italy. The Tuscan food writer Giuliano Bugialli marks the olive oil version as the original. He notes that the cream was a postwar addition.
